title: George White Anti-Lynching Bill (H.R. 6963)
summary: Representative George Henry White introduced the first federal anti-lynching bill to protect citizens against mob violence, but the measure never made it out of committee.
outcome_summary: Its failure left Black communities exposed to racial terror at the dawn of Jim Crow and showed how little federal protection Congress was willing to provide even when lynching was openly used to terrorize Black citizens.
impact_notes: Historically significant as the first federal anti-lynching effort, and significant precisely because Congress failed to act when mob violence against Black Americans was already entrenched.

scores.notes: A major missed opportunity for federal intervention against racial terror.

sources.publisher: U.S. House of Representatives, History, Art & Archives
sources.notes: Official House historical record describing George White's H.R. 6963, its introduction date, and the fact that it never left committee.

sources.publisher: U.S. House of Representatives, History, Art & Archives
sources.notes: Official historical essay explaining White's introduction of the first federal anti-lynching bill in 1900.

title: Gavagan Anti-Lynching Bill (H.R. 1507)
summary: The House passed Representative Joseph Gavagan's anti-lynching bill in 1937, but Senate filibusters and failed cloture efforts prevented it from becoming law.
outcome_summary: Its defeat showed that even after the House passed stronger anti-lynching legislation, the Senate remained willing to preserve white supremacist violence by blocking federal punishment.
impact_notes: A major failed anti-lynching measure whose defeat underscored the power of southern obstruction against Black civil-rights protection.

scores.notes: One of the most important blocked anti-lynching efforts because it actually passed the House and still died under Senate obstruction.

sources.notes: Official Senate record showing failed cloture attempts on H.R. 1507 in 1937.

sources.publisher: U.S. House of Representatives, History, Art & Archives
sources.notes: Official House history page describing the 1937 Gavagan anti-lynching bill, House passage, and Senate failure.

title: John R. Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act of 2021
summary: Would have restored and modernized key Voting Rights Act preclearance and anti-discrimination protections weakened by Shelby County v. Holder and subsequent voting-rights litigation.
outcome_summary: The House passed H.R. 4, and a Senate version followed, but the bill ultimately stalled under Senate obstruction, leaving major voting-rights protections unrestored during a period of aggressive state-level voting restrictions.
impact_notes: A central blocked voting-rights reform because it was designed specifically to rebuild federal protections after major Supreme Court retrenchment.

scores.notes: A high-stakes failed reform with direct implications for Black political participation and anti-discrimination enforcement.

sources.source_title: H.R.4 - John R. Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act of 2021
sources.notes: Official bill page showing House introduction, House passage, and Senate receipt.

sources.source_title: S.4 - John R. Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act of 2021
sources.notes: Official Senate bill page showing the November 3, 2021 failed cloture motion to proceed.

title: Ending Qualified Immunity Act
summary: Would have eliminated the qualified immunity defense in civil rights actions, making it easier to hold state actors personally liable for constitutional violations.
outcome_summary: The bill was introduced in the wake of nationwide protests over police violence but failed to advance, leaving a major barrier to civil-rights accountability in place.
impact_notes: Important not because it passed, but because its failure left intact one of the legal doctrines most criticized for impeding accountability in cases of police and state violence affecting Black communities.

scores.notes: A major failed accountability reform tied to the policing and civil-rights debate after 2020.

sources.notes: Official House bill page showing introduction and committee referral of the 117th Congress version.

title: Commission to Study and Develop Reparation Proposals for African Americans Act
summary: Would establish a federal commission to study slavery, subsequent racial discrimination, and the continuing effects of those systems, and to recommend remedies including reparative measures.
outcome_summary: The bill kept reparations in the federal legislative conversation, but it remained stuck in committee and did not create the commission needed to generate formal federal findings and recommendations.
impact_notes: A high-visibility blocked reform because it sought only a study commission, yet even that limited federal reckoning with slavery and its afterlives did not advance.

scores.notes: A major blocked reparations measure with strong symbolic and agenda-setting significance.

sources.source_title: H.R.40 - Commission to Study and Develop Reparation Proposals for African Americans Act
sources.notes: Official bill page showing the 118th Congress version and its referral to the House Judiciary Committee.
